summary: DeVault argues that the most successful replacement technologies are those that simplify rather than expand upon their predecessors. He contends that tools which start as supersets of existing software inherit their warts and struggle to fully supplant them, while those that isolate the core problem and solve it with less complexity tend to succeed. He cites Alpine Linux, musl libc, Go, UTF-8, and JSON as examples of successful simplification, while positioning zsh and Rust (relative to C) as cautionary examples of scope expansion. He concludes that questioning the premise of existing software and applying hindsight yields better replacements than building more on top of existing hacks.
key_points: Tools that expand on predecessors rather than refining them tend to fail at fully replacing them
Go succeeded by filling a niche with marked simplicity and a commitment to keeping that simplicity over time
JSON replaced XML and UTF-8 replaced the encoding zoo precisely because they were simpler and more universal
Rust is too complex to displace C or Go but simple enough relative to C++ to likely render it obsolete
The best approach to replacement is questioning the premise of existing software rather than building on top of it
money_quotes: A truly great alternative finds the nugget of truth at the center of the idea, cuts out the cruft, and solves the same problem with less.
What these projects have in common is that they expand on the ideas of their predecessors, rather than refining them.
Alpine does this by being simpler than the rest: it's the only Linux system I can fit more or less entirely in my head.
When zsh started as a superset of bash, it crippled its ability to compete on 'having less warts than bash'.
Rust has done a poor job of scope management, and is significantly more complex than many of the languages it competes with, notably C and Go.
In computing, we make do with a hodge podge of hacks and kludges which, at best, approximate the solutions to the problems that computing presents us.
If you start with one such hack as the basis of a supposed replacement and build more on top of it, you will inherit the warts.
Despite my infamous distaste for Rust, long-time readers will know that where I have distaste for Rust, I have passionate scorn for C++.
key_insight: The most successful replacements in computing are those that simplify rather than extend, finding the essential core of a problem and discarding accumulated cruft rather than building more complexity on top of it.
